title: Because They Have Laid Hands Even on Tamil-Medium Education?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The English translation follows the printed source's policy details, quotations and rhetoric. Visually confirmed OCR defects are repaired only in the derived Tamil reading copy; the canonical source layer remains unchanged.
paragraphs: When the DMK was in office from 2006 to 2011, it introduced and enacted the Tamil Nadu Tamil Learning Act to make Tamil a compulsory subject in every school. It also put an end to the situation in which students could obtain Bachelor of Arts and Bachelor of Science degrees without studying Tamil as a subject, issuing and implementing an order requiring every undergraduate student to take Tamil as a compulsory subject and pass it.

Furthermore, until the 2009-2010 academic year, university question papers for engineering degree courses were provided only in English, and answers had to be written only in English. The DMK government changed this by directing universities, from 2010-2011 onwards, to provide question papers in either English or Tamil and to permit answers to be written in English or Tamil. This scheme was particularly beneficial to students who had completed Class 12 through the Tamil medium and wished to enter engineering colleges.

Continuing this effort, paragraph 114 of the DMK government's 2010-2011 Budget announced: “The facility for students to pursue engineering degree courses with Tamil as the medium of instruction in Anna University engineering colleges will be introduced in the coming academic year.” An order was issued accordingly.

In June 2010, during the DMK government, on the distinguished occasion of the World Classical Tamil Conference held in Coimbatore, arrangements were made at the Anna Universities of Technology for students in civil and mechanical engineering to study in their mother tongue, through Tamil, for the first time in any Indian State. In that very first year of Tamil-medium engineering education, 1,378 students joined these courses in 2010-2011. The number has gradually grown, and around 4,000 young men and women are now studying in Tamil-medium engineering classes. Nor did the DMK government stop after opening those classes. During its tenure, a government order was also issued granting a 20 per cent reservation in employment to those who completed engineering degrees through Tamil.

But from 2011 onwards, under the AIADMK government that succeeded the DMK administration, no importance has been given to these Tamil-medium engineering courses. The first cohort of 1,378 students admitted to Tamil-medium engineering programmes during the DMK government is now about to complete its final year. Complaints have arisen under the present AIADMK government that private companies conducting campus interviews at university colleges not only fail to give employment preference to these Tamil-medium students, but disregard them altogether. The students are consequently incensed. Private companies come forward to select only those educated through English.

Speaking about the present situation, Tamil-medium engineering students lament: “In recruitment drives conducted by private companies at engineering colleges, only English-medium students are selected. When we question the companies about this discrimination, they say that because theirs is a foreign company, they can select only those educated in English. Although we possess the ability, we are being ignored. Even Power Grid, a Central government enterprise, follows the same approach as private companies and pushes aside those who have completed engineering through Tamil. The AIADMK government, which ought to insist upon the 20 per cent employment reservation fixed by the government for Tamil-medium engineering graduates, remains silent and does not intervene. The university administration, too, throws up its hands and says it cannot interfere in private companies' selection methods.”

The students further express their anguish: “Once we complete engineering through Tamil, the Tamil Nadu government must provide us employment immediately, or offer us employment through the Tamil Nadu Public Service Commission. The State government must explain its position to us. Otherwise, we will be forced to agitate. Only if employment opportunities are created for us, the first students to graduate with engineering degrees through Tamil, will other students be encouraged to continue joining Tamil-medium engineering programmes. If not, Tamil-medium engineering education will become a mere dream.”
But whether the AIADMK government will resolve these fundamental problems remains a question!
For this government, words rich in meaning and beauty such as Tamil, classical language, Tamil-medium education and equitable education all belong to the category of “allergens.”

On 10 May 2013, during the debate on demands for grants for School Education in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, the AIADMK government's School Education Minister announced: “During the last academic year, two English-medium sections each were opened in Classes 1 and 6 at 320 government schools. A total of 640 sections were opened, and 22,400 students are studying in them. Continuing this, from the coming academic year, English-medium sections will be opened in every government primary, middle, high and higher-secondary school where there is demand. This will benefit 150,000 students each year.” The AIADMK School Education Minister made this announcement without knowing even the alphabet of the Dravidian movement's long history concerning Tamil as the medium of instruction.

The belief that knowledge develops only through education in English is merely a state of mind; it contains not the slightest truth. Education in one's mother tongue creates the opportunity for independent thought to flourish. Education in another language becomes a major obstacle to independent thought. Mahatma Gandhi, Perarignar Anna and every socially concerned leader, along with educationists and thinkers, have stressed the necessity and importance of education in the mother tongue.

In many countries across the world, English is not even a commonly spoken language. In Russia, Germany, Japan, China and many other countries, their own national languages serve both as the language of administration and as the medium of instruction.

It is not an acceptable course for government schools to declare English the medium of instruction merely to attract students in competition with commercially run private schools. Since equitable education came into force, private and government schools have stood on an equal footing as far as the syllabus is concerned. The proper arrangement for transforming government schools into advanced centres of learning is therefore to create and expand basic infrastructure in them, as in the best private schools, and provide quality education. Likewise, instead of dismissing Tamil-medium engineering out of resentment merely because it was introduced under the DMK government, continuing to encourage it is the only way to lay the foundation for a better future society. English-medium education will turn into an obsession with English and gradually become a regressive measure that throws the doors wide open to the privatisation of education.

Rather than carry forward the valuable efforts undertaken by the DMK government for Tamil as the medium of instruction, the AIADMK government's minister made an announcement in the Assembly that would dig a pit and bury them all. At a time when progressive demands are gaining strength that Tamil be declared and implemented as one of the Union government's official languages, that every Central government employment examination be conducted in Tamil as well, and that Tamil be accepted as a language of proceedings in the Madras High Court, suddenly introducing English-medium education in government schools and disregarding Tamil-medium engineering students is like stumbling into needless trouble by provoking a sleeping tiger.

Supporters of the mother tongue are deeply distressed to see the AIADMK government foster the attitude that English is not merely a language, but the sole path to knowledge. Whether at school or higher levels, extending mother-tongue instruction can bring a society only great benefits. Countless studies around the world have examined which approach to the medium of instruction is correct, appropriate and scientifically sound. Most findings demonstrate that mother-tongue education is the best. UNESCO, the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization, formulated in 1951, has emphasised mother-tongue education. Every education commission constituted by the Indian and State governments has established its superiority.

It is painful to see certain politicians in Tamil Nadu, who conduct themselves as though they alone were born for Tamil, keep their mouths shut on this important issue for fear that speaking might incur the Chief Minister's anger.

It is only to conceal all this behind a curtain and divert attention that, before Chief Minister Jayalalithaa left for Kodanad, the government's Information Department supplied the newspapers, three days late, with reports that she had remotely laid the foundation stone for a new building of the International Institute of Tamil Studies from the Secretariat and inaugurated a Tirukkural art gallery built at a cost of Rs. 10 lakh. From this alone, one can understand how much importance the AIADMK government's Information Department attaches to international Tamil research and the Tirukkural. Those who read the report can also clearly understand how Valluvar Kottam has been neglected under this government and become a site of antisocial activity; how the 133-foot statue of Tiruvalluvar at Kanniyakumari has been left without proper maintenance; and how institutions opened under the DMK government, including Semmozhi Poonga, Tolkappiya Poonga, the Pavendhar Classical Tamil Research Library and the Central Institute of Classical Tamil, are being allowed to deteriorate!

What has become of all the libraries established in each of Tamil Nadu's 12,680 village panchayats under the DMK government's Anna Renaissance Scheme, bearing the name “Tiruvalluvar Reading Room”? Can a library bearing Tiruvalluvar's name be permitted under “Amma's” rule? The AIADMK government must come forward to create employment for the thousands of students studying Tamil-medium engineering, in accordance with the reservation already announced by the government. If, out of its usual “resentment” because Tamil-medium engineering was introduced under the DMK government, it fails to provide those students government employment immediately, the government must heed this warning: it will bear the blame for turning them all into protesters, just as they have announced.
Affectionately,
M.K.
28 December 2013
